nativeRange.habitat: Streams and seasonally flooded watercourses of the Western Ghats, where it grows emergent and submerged along rocky margins

decorPreferences: driftwood or rocks for epiphytic attachment
nutrient-rich substrate when planted in the ground
open midground space to show off lacy leaf form
moderate to strong flow keeps leaves clean

summary: Hygrophila Pinnatifida is a distinctive stem plant from the Western Ghats of India, recognized by its deeply lobed, fern-like leaves with burgundy-red undersides. Unusually among stem plants, it can be rooted in substrate or attached to driftwood and rocks, where it spreads by lateral runners.
careNotes: Hygrophila Pinnatifida is a striking and unusually versatile stem plant: the deeply lobed, fern-like leaves with bronze-to-burgundy undersides are distinctive, and the plant can be cultivated either rooted in substrate or attached epiphytically to driftwood and rocks. Its lateral stems develop adventitious roots (fine roots that emerge along the stem and grip hardscape), allowing it to colonize wood and stone much as Bucephalandra or Anubias does. Light demand is medium to high; the brightest red-burgundy coloration and tightest, most compact growth with strong lateral branching develop under high light. The plant is reportedly susceptible to potassium deficiency, which shows as pinprick holes in older leaves — keeping macronutrient and trace fertilization adequate is important to prevent this. Substrate type is relatively unimportant since the species develops only shallow roots; nutrient-rich aquasoil benefits planted specimens but is unnecessary for epiphytic culture. It is best displayed in small groups in the midground or attached to a hardscape feature, where the lacy leaf shape stands out against simpler-leaved plants. While generally regarded as a reasonably adaptable plant, achieving the signature red coloration and compact form reliably requires high light, CO2 supplementation, and balanced fertilization, which places it at an intermediate care level.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward: trim healthy top cuttings or lateral runners and either replant in substrate or attach to hardscape with glue, thread, or fishing line until lateral roots anchor it. Runner shoots and daughter plants established at the parent's nodes tend to establish faster than bare cuttings. Regular topping and replanting also encourages bushier, denser stands.

primary.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
primary.notes: Primary source. Provides taxonomy (Acanthaceae), endemic Western Ghats range, full water parameter envelope (18-28°C, pH 5-8, kH 2-12 dKH, CO2 20-40 mg/L), medium-to-high light, medium growth, 5-40 cm height, 10-30 cm width, propagation by runners/cuttings/daughter plants, and the potassium-deficiency pinprick-hole note.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Acanthaceae), Indian origin, pH 6-7.5, medium-to-high light, CO2 recommended, moderate growth, variable leaf coloration (brown/purple/red/green/yellow depending on conditions), submerged or emersed cultivation, propagation by stem cuttings and lateral branches, and the potassium-deficiency observation.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (15-40 cm tall, 10-20 cm wide), moderate growth rate, epiphytic flexibility (substrate or attached to rock/driftwood), and red coloration development under high light. Confirms classification as a stem plant suited to high-tech aquariums.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked Indian origin, temperature 60-82°F (≈16-28°C), moderate-to-high light, optional CO2, medium growth, midground/background/epiphyte placement, propagation by trimmed top cuttings, and the lacy pinnate leaf form with olive-green tops and dark green to burgundy undersides.
